title: trAIt: Species-by-Trait Data Retrieval using Large Language Models
abstract: Biological research often requires information about species’ traits. Manual literature collation can be time-consuming and miss parts of the literature. To address this gap, we developed trAIt , a publicly available software for the retrieval of characteristics of species from scientific literature catalogued in the Europe PubMed Central (PubMed) database. trAIt provides a graphical user interface (GUI) in which users specify species and characteristics of interest. Leveraging a large language model (LLM), trAIt retrieves relevant papers, combines their content through a consensus-based summarization model, and outputs a species-by-characteristic table. For a case study involving frog species, trAIt recovered 47.1% of trait-species combinations in 2.75 hours, while an expert curator independently recovered 62.4% over months. The consensus-based summarization substantially aids accuracy compared to single-source extraction. Across three case studies of vertebrate taxa, an expert confirmed the accuracy of 70.9% of trait-species entries recovered by trAIt . We observed considerable variation across taxa in trAIt ’s accuracy, which is possibly due to heterogeneity in open-access literature availability and inconsistencies in species and trait terminology. In sum, our analysis suggests that LLM-based tools can accelerate biological data synthesis but should be used to support domain experts’ research, rather than replace their judgment.

benchmark_justification: The tool's performance is compared to human experts, but lacks extensive benchmarking against other AI tools.

paper_title: trAIt: Species-by-Trait Data Retrieval using Large Language Models

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to develop trAIt, a software tool for the retrieval of species-by-trait data from scientific literature using large language models, aiming to streamline the process of data collection in biological research.
objective.evidence: To address this gap, we developed trAIt, a publicly available software for the retrieval of characteristics of species from scientific literature catalogued in the Europe PubMed Central (PubMed) database.

knowledge_gap.answer: The paper addresses the gap in efficient retrieval of species trait data from scientific literature, which is often time-consuming and prone to missing parts of the literature.
knowledge_gap.evidence: Biological research often requires information about species’ traits. Manual literature collation can be time-consuming and miss parts of the literature.

answer: Development of trAIt, a tool that uses large language models to retrieve and summarize species-by-trait data from scientific literature.
Implementation of a consensus-based summarization model to improve accuracy over single-source extraction.
Integration of a graphical user interface for user-friendly interaction with the tool.
evidence: We developed trAIt, a trait-by-species data retrieval software. trAIt queries scientific literature and leverages a large language model (LLM) to extract concise information from large bodies of text.
The consensus-based summarization substantially aids accuracy compared to single-source extraction.
trAIt provides a graphical user interface (GUI) in which users specify species and characteristics of interest.

inspirational_papers.answer: - The Europe PMC Consortium (2015) Europe PMC: a full-text literature database for the life sciences and platform for innovation. (Methodological precursors)
inspirational_papers.evidence: trAIt retrieves relevant papers for that species and trait through PubMed’s open-access API.

steps.step: User uploads species and trait data.
steps.input: Spreadsheet with species names and traits, text file with trait descriptions.
steps.output: Prepared input files for trAIt processing.
steps.evidence: The user uploads a spreadsheet listing species names and traits of interest, along with a text file describing each trait in plain language.
For each species-trait pair, trAIt retrieves relevant papers for that species and trait through PubMed’s open-access API.

steps.step: Process and filter text from retrieved papers.
steps.evidence: trAIt downloads retrieved papers in PDF format and converts them to raw text.
For each retrieved paper, trAIt applies a standardized extraction prompt to the LLM, supplying the paper text and trait definition.

steps.input: Extracted trait values from multiple papers.
steps.evidence: If a query returns multiple candidate values from multiple papers, trAIt reconciles and standardizes the results into a single, unified output.

steps.output: Structured CSV file with trait data.
steps.evidence: The aggregated results are written to a structured CSV file.

tools.name: Large Language Model (LLM)
tools.description: Used for extracting trait information from text.
tools.evidence: trAIt queries scientific literature and leverages a large language model (LLM) to extract concise information from large bodies of text.

tools.name: PubMed API
tools.evidence: trAIt retrieves relevant papers for that species and trait through PubMed’s open-access API.

evaluation_metrics.name: Completeness
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the fraction of species-trait answers retrieved.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate the retrieval performance of trAIt.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Completeness varied considerably across inputs—50.6%, 60.1%, and 26.6% for Mixed Vertebrates, Birds and Frogs, respectively.

evaluation_metrics.name: Accuracy
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the correctness of retrieved trait values.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to assess the accuracy of trAIt's output compared to manually curated reference values.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Accuracy showed similar distributions across traits within each input.

methods.name: Input and Data Collection from the User
methods.evidence: The user uploads a spreadsheet listing species names and traits of interest, along with a text file describing each trait in plain language.

methods.name: Literature or Dataset Retrieval
methods.evidence: For each species-trait pair, trAIt retrieves relevant papers for that species and trait through PubMed’s open-access API.

methods.name: Knowledge Extraction and Structurization
methods.evidence: trAIt applies a standardized extraction prompt to the LLM, supplying the paper text and trait definition, and receiving the model’s response corresponding to the requested trait.

performance_summary.summary: trAIt achieved an accuracy of 71.6% on the Mixed Vertebrate input, 57.0% on the Bird input, and 84.0% on the Frog input.
performance_summary.evidence: Among the three inputs, trAIt achieved an accuracy of 71.6% on the Mixed Vertebrate input, 57.0% on the Bird input, and 84.0% on the Frog input.

evaluation_metrics.name: Completeness
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the fraction of species-trait answers retrieved.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate the retrieval performance of trAIt.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Completeness varied considerably across inputs—50.6%, 60.1%, and 26.6% for Mixed Vertebrates, Birds and Frogs, respectively.

evaluation_metrics.name: Accuracy
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the correctness of retrieved trait values.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to assess the accuracy of trAIt's output compared to manually curated reference values.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Accuracy showed similar distributions across traits within each input.

limitations.name: Limited Literature Availability
limitations.description: trAIt's performance is constrained by the availability of open-access literature, affecting completeness and accuracy.
limitations.evidence: We hypothesized that this is a result of the limited availability of open-access papers on many of the species in the Frog input.

limitations.name: Text-Only Extraction
limitations.description: trAIt cannot extract data from figures or supplementary materials, which limits its ability to retrieve complete information.
limitations.evidence: trAIt relies on data being mineable as text rather than in figures, files or other supplementary materials.

future_directions.name: Integration of Taxon-Specific Databases
future_directions.description: Explore the integration of databases and primary literature in parallel to gather trait data.
future_directions.evidence: Ideally, trAIt would integrate databases and primary literature in parallel to gather trait data.

future_directions.name: Improving Literature Access
future_directions.description: Implement an authentication system to allow access to subscription-based papers.
future_directions.evidence: To improve literature availability, implementing an authentication system would allow a user with institutional access to retrieve access-restricted papers.

resource_link.evidence: Our code base is publicly available at: https://github.com/harpak-lab/trAIt
